editableNewDialogue.text: Can you finish and polish this sentence: They advised us recently that they will be closing on a sale of one of the storage sites that is part of Canadian Commercial Ventures (noted in their attached net worth statement) within the coming weeks. His portion of the deal is expected to be over $1 million dollars, so they wanted to find out the most tax efficient way to put this funds in use.

They recently advised us that they expect to close the sale of one of the storage sites held by Canadian Commercial Ventures (as noted in their attached net worth statement) within the coming weeks. His share of the proceeds is expected to exceed $1 million, and they would like to determine the most tax-efficient way to put these funds to use.
Should it be “close the sale” or “close on the sale”?

Both are used, but they’re slightly different: “close the sale” is a general phrasing, while “close on the sale” is common in real estate and emphasizes the closing process. In this context, either works—if you want a slightly more formal, general-business tone, use “close the sale.”
Got it. Also, does “over $1 million dollars” sound redundant?
Yes—use either “over $1 million” or “over one million dollars,” but not both. “Over $1 million” is the most concise.

Please tweak this for clarity: They advised us recently that they will be closing on a sale of one of the storage sites that is part of Canadian Commercial Ventures (noted in their attached net worth statement) within the coming weeks. His portion of the deal is expected to be over $1 million dollars, so they wanted to find out the most tax efficient way to put this funds in use.

Here’s a revised version for clarity and grammar:

"They recently advised us that they expect to close, within the coming weeks, on the sale of one of the storage sites held under Canadian Commercial Ventures (as noted in their attached net worth statement). His share of the proceeds is expected to exceed $1 million, and they would like to determine the most tax-efficient way to put these funds to use."
Just to clarify—I don’t oversee all our clients’ accounts. I help out here and there when needed, but I’m not the one overseeing everyone’s accounts.
Understood. So rather than overseeing all clients’ accounts, you support the team as needed and step in when help is required.
